title: Génoise sponge
subtitle: The classic French whole-egg whisked sponge with clarified butter
excerpt: A light, fine-crumbed French sponge made by whisking whole eggs with sugar over heat until thick, then folding in flour and clarified butter. Dryer than a British sponge and intended to be soaked with syrup.
sourceNotes: Génoise is named after Genoa, though it became central to French classical patisserie. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), Project Gutenberg, gives the génoise as a foundational sponge for layer cakes, petits fours, and charlottes. The whole-egg foam method distinguishes it from the separated-egg sponge; the addition of clarified butter enriches the crumb without deflating it.

content.text: About génoise sponge

Génoise is the workhorse sponge of French patisserie. Unlike a British Victoria sponge, it is not rich enough to eat plain; it is made to be soaked with sugar syrup, then layered or rolled. The foam is made with whole eggs rather than separated whites, which gives a finer, denser crumb with good structure. A small amount of
clarified butter
folded in at the end enriches the texture without deflating the foam.
The key step is whisking the eggs and sugar over gentle heat to the
ribbon stage

. The warmth helps the eggs incorporate more air and the finished foam is stable enough to hold the flour and butter without collapsing. Do not let the mixture get too hot; the eggs should feel warm to the touch, not hot.
Ingredients

attrs.prepNote: clarified: melt gently and skim off the white foam, use only the clear yellow butter

content.text: Method
Prepare and warm the eggs

Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease and line a 20 cm round tin. Bring a small pan of water to a simmer. Put {{eggs}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} in a large heatproof bowl and set it over the pan without letting the base touch the water. Whisk constantly by hand for about four minutes until the mixture is warm to the touch, around 40°C. Remove from the heat.
Whisk to ribbon stage

Whisk on high speed (electric whisk or stand mixer) for 8 to 10 minutes until the mixture is very pale, doubled in volume, and at the ribbon stage: a trail lifted from the whisk holds its shape for five seconds before dissolving. Meanwhile, melt {{unsalted-butter}} gently, skim the foam, and keep the clarified butter warm.
Fold in the flour and butter

Sift {{plain-flour}} over the foam in two additions, folding with a large metal spoon each time using a light cutting motion. After the second addition, drizzle the warm clarified {{unsalted-butter}} around the edge and fold it in with the fewest strokes possible. Work quickly; over-folding loses volume.
Bake

Pour the batter into the tin from a height to break any large bubbles. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes until the surface is golden, springs back when pressed, and a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin for five minutes, then turn out onto a rack. An oven thermometer is useful; a high oven can set the outside before the centre is cooked.
Notes

To use as a layer cake base, split the sponge into two or three layers. Brush each layer with sugar syrup (equal weights sugar and water, heated to dissolve). Fill with crème mousseline or whipped cream.
Cold clarified butter deflates the foam immediately on contact. It must be warm (not hot) when you add it.
Génoise tastes dry if eaten without syrup. The dryness is by design: it absorbs syrup without becoming soggy and holds its shape under the weight of filling and decoration.
